summary: The :core-settings module is well-structured and implements all spec requirements correctly. Proto schema, serializer, repository, and Hilt wiring are sound. Six TDD anchors are present and well-asserted. Three findings require attention: a critical misuse of the Hilt plugin alias (hilt.application applied to a library module), a major deviation from the documented ErrorLog component whitelist (patterns.md lists only 4 components; 'settings-io' is not yet enrolled â€” cross-task dependency on D27/Task 3), and a minor DataStore parent-directory creation concern. The patterns.md Hilt module shape deviation (abstract class vs object) is intentional and technically necessary for @Binds. Smoke path drift (generated proto path) is noted for documentation. No Compose/Activity imports found in main source â€” module boundary TAC-8 respected.

findings.title: Wrong Hilt Gradle plugin alias applied to a library module
findings.description: Line 7 applies `alias(libs.plugins.hilt.application)` â€” this is the `com.google.dagger.hilt.android` plugin variant meant exclusively for the `:app` (Android Application) module. A library module must use `com.google.dagger.hilt.android.library` (commonly aliased as `hilt.library` or declared separately). Using the application plugin on a library module typically produces a Gradle configuration error or silently misconfigures the Hilt annotation-processing graph, which can cause Dagger component generation to fail at compile time or produce incorrect component graphs in consumers. Looking at the version catalog diff, only one hilt plugin alias exists (`hilt-application`), confirming no library-specific alias was added. `CoreRuntimeModule` in `:core-runtime` uses the same alias â€” if that module currently compiles, it may be a pre-existing issue masked by the project structure, but it is incorrect per Hilt documentation and should be corrected here to avoid silent misconfigurations as the project grows.
findings.recommendation: Add a `hilt-library = { id = "com.google.dagger.hilt.android.library", version.ref = "hilt" }` entry to `gradle/libs.versions.toml` and replace `alias(libs.plugins.hilt.application)` with `alias(libs.plugins.hilt.library)` in `core-settings/build.gradle.kts`. Apply the same fix to `core-runtime/build.gradle.kts` if it uses the same alias. The application plugin should only appear in `:app/build.gradle.kts`.

findings.title: ErrorLog component 'settings-io' not in patterns.md whitelist â€” cross-task dependency on D27
findings.description: patterns.md Â§ ErrorLog component strings explicitly states: 'Only these values are allowed as the component argument to ErrorLog.e(): "download", "inference-init", "inference", "inference-cleanup". Adding a new component requires updating the whitelist in the reviewing spec and the code audit.' The constant ERROR_COMPONENT = "settings-io" at line 14 introduces a new component that is NOT in the current whitelist. ErrorLog.kt itself does not enforce the whitelist in code â€” it passes component strings as-is â€” so there is no runtime crash. However, the patterns constraint is violated and future Task 3 (tech-spec D27) is supposed to add whitelist enforcement to ErrorLog.kt. When that enforcement lands, existing callers using 'settings-io' will break unless D27 also adds 'settings-io' to the closed set. This is a documented cross-task dependency, not a standalone blocker, but it should be flagged now so Task 3 explicitly includes 'settings-io' (along with 'camera', 'audio', 'attachment-decode') in its whitelist extension.
findings.recommendation: Flag in decisions.md or Task 3 notes: Task 3 (D27 enforcement) MUST add 'settings-io' to the ErrorLog whitelist. Optionally add a TODO comment at the constant: `// Cross-task dep: D27 (Task 3) adds 'settings-io' to ErrorLog whitelist`. No code change needed now â€” the component value is correct per tech-spec; whitelist enforcement is a Task 3 deliverable.

findings.title: DataStore file path relies on DataStoreFactory auto-creating parent dir â€” undocumented behavior
findings.description: The produceFile lambda returns `File(context.filesDir, "datastore/app_settings.pb")`. The parent directory `context.filesDir/datastore/` is not explicitly created before DataStoreFactory.create is called. DataStoreFactory does call `parentFile.mkdirs()` internally in its file-creation path (verified in AOSP source for datastore 1.1.x), so this works in practice. However, this relies on an implementation detail of DataStoreFactory that is not part of its documented public contract. If a future version changes this behavior, the DataStore will silently fail on first write with a FileNotFoundException. The tech-spec shared resources table documents the path as `context.filesDir/datastore/app_settings.pb`, confirming the intent.
findings.recommendation: Make parent-directory creation explicit in the lambda: `produceFile = { File(context.filesDir, "datastore/app_settings.pb").also { it.parentFile?.mkdirs() } }`. This is defensive, self-documenting, and has zero cost.

findings.title: ioException_observeDoesNotCrash: asserts null but corrupt proto triggers CorruptionException path, not IOException path
findings.description: The test writes corrupt bytes to a proto file and expects `observePerModelSettings` to return null rather than throw. The corrupt bytes cause `AppSettings.parseFrom` to throw `InvalidProtocolBufferException`, which AppSettingsSerializer wraps as `CorruptionException`. The `.catch` block in `DefaultAppSettingsRepository.observePerModelSettings` only catches `IOException` (line ~25 of DefaultAppSettingsRepository.kt): `if (cause is IOException) { ... emit(AppSettings.getDefaultInstance()) } else { throw cause }`. Since `CorruptionException extends IOException` (androidx.datastore.core.CorruptionException extends java.io.IOException), the catch does cover this path â€” but the relationship is non-obvious and worth verifying. Additionally, the test name says 'ioException' but the actual failure path is CorruptionException. The test also asserts `assertNull(observed)` but the `.catch` emits `AppSettings.getDefaultInstance()` (not a signal to return null); since the map key 'any-model' is not in the default instance, the `.map` step returns null â€” this is correct but the chain of reasoning is subtle and should be documented.
findings.recommendation: Add a comment in the test explaining the exception chain: corrupt bytes â†’ InvalidProtocolBufferException â†’ CorruptionException (extends IOException) â†’ caught by .catch â†’ emits defaultInstance â†’ map returns null. Rename the test to `corruptionException_observeReturnsNull` to accurately describe what is being tested. Optionally add a companion test `ioException_onSave_swallowsAndLogs` to explicitly cover the IOException path in savePerModelSettings (currently untested).

findings.title: Real ErrorLog in tests writes to Robolectric filesDir â€” potential cross-test side effects
findings.description: setUp() at line 55-58 constructs a real `ErrorLog(context)` using the Robolectric ApplicationProvider context. ErrorLog writes to `context.filesDir/logs/errors.log` on disk (Robolectric maps this to a temp directory per test run). If ErrorLog.e is called during the ioException_observeDoesNotCrash test, it performs actual I/O. Robolectric's filesDir is shared across all tests in the same process run unless isolated. This is low-risk in practice (Robolectric resets app state between tests), but the test's setUp creates a new ErrorLog instance per test while the underlying file path is shared. If tests run concurrently (unlikely with default JUnit 4 single-thread runner but possible with JUnit 5 parallel or future config), this could cause flakiness.
findings.recommendation: Replace the real ErrorLog with a no-op fake or a simple spy in tests that do not explicitly assert logging behavior: create a `FakeErrorLog` inner class implementing the same interface, or pass a mock/stub. For tests that DO assert log behavior (none currently, but future ones may), use a real ErrorLog with an explicitly isolated temp directory. This keeps tests hermetic and avoids accidental I/O.

findings.title: Smoke step proto path in task.md does not match actual generated output path
findings.description: The smoke verification step at line 86 of task.md instructs: `ls core-settings/build/generated/source/proto/main/java/...`. The actual generated output path for the protobuf Gradle plugin 0.9.5 configured with `generateProtoTasks { all().forEach { task -> task.plugins { id("java") { option("lite") } } } }` is `build/generated/sources/proto/debug/java/...` (note: 'sources' plural, and 'debug' build variant instead of 'main'). This is a known path change between protobuf plugin versions. The smoke step will fail with 'No such file or directory' when executed literally, potentially causing confusion during verification.
findings.recommendation: Update the smoke step in task.md to use the correct path: `ls core-settings/build/generated/sources/proto/debug/java/app/sanctum/machina/core/settings/proto/`. Also note this in decisions.md so future tasks referencing the generated path use the correct form.
